title: Today's AI Brief: Google I/O showed how the path for AI-driven science is shifting
summary: Google I/O keynote highlights AI-driven science, shifting paths and smarter interfaces
imageAlt: Image from source article for Google I/O showed how the path for AI-driven science is shifting
imageCaption: Google I/O showed how the path for AI-driven science is shifting
imageSourceTitle: Google I/O showed how the path for AI-driven science is shifting

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI Brief. I am looking at some fresh signals from the world of artificial intelligence, and I've got a couple of interesting updates to share with you today. It's always exciting to see how AI is evolving, and this week is no exception.

First up, Google I/O showed us how the path for AI-driven science is shifting. During the keynote, Demis Hassabis, the CEO of Google DeepMind, made a striking statement: we're currently 'standing in the foothills of the singularity.' For those who might not be familiar, the singularity refers to a hypothetical point in time when AI surpasses human intelligence. It's a concept that's both fascinating and a bit unsettling. I'm not sure about you, but I find it intriguing to think about what that could mean for our future.

Next, I've been reading about the future of physical AI. It seems that the focus isn't on creating smarter robots, but rather on developing smarter interfaces. Think about it like this: instead of building a robot that can do everything, we're working on making it easier for humans and robots to work together. A field technician on a wind turbine, for example, needs to send a command to a diagnostic device. Smarter interfaces can make that process much more efficient. It's all about collaboration and making our lives easier, one interface at a time.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
